metadata.name: papermodels
classifiers: License :: OSI Approved :: Apache Software License

functions.name: papermodels.fileio.utils.str_to_int
documentation:
Returns the string 's' converted into an integer if possible.
Returns the original string, 's', otherwise.

functions.name: papermodels.fileio.utils.str_to_float
documentation:
Returns the string 's' converted into a float if possible.
Returns the original string, 's', otherwise.

functions.name: papermodels.fileio.utils.read_csv_file
documentation:
Returns data contained in the file, 'filename' as a list of lists.
It is assumed that the data in the file is "csv-ish", meaning with
comma-separated values.

functions.name: papermodels.fileio.model_files.get_structured_beam_data
documentation: Returns a dictionary that has string keys describing the attributes of a beam for analysis.

functions.name: papermodels.fileio.model_files.parse_beam_attributes
documentation:
Returns a dictionary of beam attributes according to which attributes are present
in 'beam_attributes' in the order according to the beam file format BEAM_FORMAT.md,
Workbook_04 edition. The order of attributes are as follows: Length,E,Iz,[Iy,A,J,nu,rho]

functions.name: papermodels.fileio.model_files.parse_kwargs
documentation:
Returns a dictionary of any of the elements in 'attr_items' that
contain an "=" character as key, value pairs.

functions.name: papermodels.fileio.model_files.map_beam_attributes
documentation:
Returns a list of str data that represents the beam attributes in the
original attributes line with any kwargs remove and placed in the expected
positional order.

functions.name: papermodels.fileio.model_files.parse_supports
documentation:
Returns a dictionary representing the data in 'supports' separated
out into a dictionary with support locations as keys and support types
as values.

Assumes 'supports' is in a format that looks like this:
['support_loc:support_type', 'support_loc:support_type', etc...]
e.g. ['1000:P', '3800:R', '6000:R']
Where the valid support types are one of: P (pinned), F (fixed), or R (roller)

functions.name: papermodels.fileio.model_files.convert_to_units
documentation:
Returns a nested list of floats representing all of the numeric string data in
'raw_data' being converted into a floats.

If the data cannot be converted, a ValueError will be raised.

functions.name: papermodels.fileio.model_files.parse_loads
documentation:
Returns a the loads in 'loads_data' structured into a list of dicts.
This implementation is just one implementation among many. I do not
make claims about it being "the best" implementation :)

functions.name: papermodels.fileio.model_files.calculate_line_load_from_trib_width
documentation:
Returns the equivalent line load for an 'area_load'for the given
trib_width. Units of quantities provided must be consistent for correct results.

'area_load' - a quantity in force/area
'trib_width_start' - a quantity of length in the same units as area. If only 'trib_width_start'
    is provided, then the line load is assumed to be uniform.
'trib_width_end' - a quantity of length in the same units as area.

functions.name: papermodels.fileio.model_files.remove_comments
documentation:
Returns 'raw_data' but with any comments removed. A comment is represented
the same as in Python with a "#" followed by arbitrary characters.

functions.name: papermodels.fileio.model_files.get_node_locations
documentation:
Returns a dict representing the node names and node coordinates required for
a beam with support locations at 'support_locations' and a beam of length of
'beam_length'.

Each node name and node location is unique. Nodes are numbered sequentially from
left to right starting at "N0".

functions.name: papermodels.fileio.model_files.extract_data
documentation: Returns the data in 'data_line' located at 'index'.

functions.name: papermodels.fileio.model_files.get_spans
documentation:
Returns the length of the backspan ("b") and the length of the cantilever ("a")
given a total beam length and the location of the cantilever support.

This function assumes that the backspan support is located at 0.0

functions.name: papermodels.fileio.model_files.get_nodes_location_fixity
documentation: Returns the node location and fixity for all nodes in 'beam_model'

functions.name: papermodels.fileio.model_files.get_all_node_reactions
documentation:
Returns a nested dictionary of node reactions for all load combos keyed
by node name, direction, and then load combo name.

functions.name: papermodels.fileio.model_files.convert_to_numeric
documentation:
Returns a nested list of floats representing all of the numeric string data in
'raw_data' being converted into a floats.

If the data cannot be converted, a ValueError will be raised.

functions.name: papermodels.paper.fdf.read_annotations
documentation:
Returns a list of Annotation objects representing the data
contained in 'fdf_file'.

'fdf_file' can either be a str or pathlib.Path

functions.name: papermodels.paper.fdf._read_fdf_file
documentation: Reads the FDF file and returns a list of strings.

functions.name: papermodels.paper.fdf._get_annotations_from_fdf
documentation:
Separates FDF data by objects
#TODO This currently only works with FDF files formatted by Bluebeam Revu.
# FDF Files created by programs with nicer formatting, like Qoppa PDF Studio,
# use different line break locations which nicely breaks this whole function.
# Will have to invest resources into reading PDF data directly via pikepdf
# or something.

functions.name: papermodels.paper.fdf._extract_type_and_vertices
documentation:
Returns a tuple of two strings representing the annotation type and
a string of vertices in the annotation type extracted from 'line',
a str of FDF data.
If 'line' does not include an annotation with vertices, None is returned.

functions.name: papermodels.paper.fdf._extract_stream_properties
documentation:
Returns a dict of properties which are available from the stream data:
'line_color', 'fill_color', 'line_weight'.

functions.name: papermodels.paper.fdf._parse_line_color
documentation:
Returns a tuple representing the parsed line color specification contained
within 'stream_line', a line of text representing the FDF data stream.
Returns None if no line color data is found in 'stream_line'

The returned tuple is in the format of (R, G, B) where each of R, G, B are a float
from 0.0 to 1.0

functions.name: papermodels.paper.fdf._parse_fill_color
documentation:
Returns a tuple representing the parsed line color specification contained
within 'stream_line', a line of text representing the FDF data stream.
Returns None if no fill color data is found in 'stream_line'

The returned tuple is in the format of (R, G, B) where each of R, G, B are a float
from 0.0 to 1.0

functions.name: papermodels.paper.fdf._parse_line_weight
documentation:
Returns a float representing the parsed line weight specification contained
within 'stream_line', a line of text representing the FDF data stream.
Returns None if no line weight is found in 'stream_line'

The returned value represents a line weight in points (1 point = 1/72 of an inch)

functions.name: papermodels.paper.fdf._parse_line_type
documentation:
Returns a tuple representing the parsed line type specification contained
within 'stream_line', a line of text representing the FDF data stream.
Returns None if no line type data is found in 'stream_line'

functions.name: papermodels.paper.fdf.scale_object
documentation:
Scale the annotation. Each vertex in 'annot' will be multiplied
by 'scale'

functions.name: papermodels.paper.annotations.annotations_to_shapely
documentation:
Returns a shapely geometry representing the geometry in 'annot'
'annots' - a list of Annotation objects
'as_geometry_collection' - If True, the geometries in 'annots'
    will all be grouped together in a shapely.geometry.GeometryCollection

functions.name: papermodels.paper.annotations.annotation_to_shapely
documentation:
Returns an shapely geometry created from the annotation type and
vertices in 'annot'.

functions.name: papermodels.paper.annotations.get_annotation_geometry_pairs
documentation: Returns a dict of annotation, shapely geometry pairs

functions.name: papermodels.paper.annotations.parse_annotations
documentation:
Returns a dictionary of annotations organized by their legend entry. If the annotation type is not
in the 'legend', then it is excluded from the results.

functions.name: papermodels.paper.annotations.tag_parsed_annotations
documentation:
Adds an identifying tag to the annotation based on the page number of the annotation and
its identified type.

functions.name: papermodels.paper.annotations.get_structural_elements
documentation:
Returns a list of Element generated from the annotations in 'annots' according to the element
types described in the 'legend'. If an annotation is not described in the legend then it will
not be included in the result list of Elements.

functions.name: papermodels.paper.annotations.get_geometry_intersections
documentation: Returns a dictionary of

functions.name: papermodels.paper.annotations.get_geometry_correspondents
documentation:
Returns a copy of 'tagged_annotations' with a 'correspondents' field added to that
attributes dictionary of each Annotation key.

functions.name: papermodels.paper.annotations._annotation_to_wkt
documentation:
Returns a WKT string representing the geometry in 'annot'. The WKT
string can be loaded with shapely.wkt.loads (see shapely documentation)

functions.name: papermodels.paper.annotations.filter_annotations
documentation:
Returns a list of annotations from 'annots' that have properties that match
the keywords in 'properties'.
Note: The filtering process currently requires that both the keys AND values in 'properties'
be hashable.
'properties' is a dictionary of annotation properties and their values, e.g.
    {'line_weight': 3.0, 'line_color': (1, 0, 0)}
    or
    {'text': "Slab Outline"}
The returned annotations will only be annotations that match ALL of the properties
described.

functions.name: papermodels.paper.annotations.scale_annotations
documentation:
Scale the annotations in 'annots'. Each vertex in each annotation in 'annots' will be multiplied
by 'scale'.
If 'paper_origin' is provided, then the annotation coordinates will have their origin reset
to 'paper_origin'. Note that 'paper_origin' is the unscaled coordinate space (i.e. in points)
The purpose of setting 'paper_origin' if the annotation has a "datum" that is set somewhere in the
file. Leave as None if there is no datum set.

functions.name: papermodels.paper.annotations.scale_vertices
documentation:
Scale the annotation. Each vertex in 'annot' will be multiplied
by 'scale'.
If 'paper_origin' is provided, then the annotation coordinates will have their origin reset
to 'paper_origin'. Note that 'paper_origin' is the unscaled coordinate space (i.e. in points)

functions.name: papermodels.paper.annotations.annotations_by_page
documentation: Returns 'annots' in a dictionary keyed by page number

functions.name: papermodels.paper.annotations._translate_vertices
documentation: Returns a list of float representing 'verticies' translated by 'offset_x' and 'offset_y'.

functions.name: papermodels.paper.annotations._group_vertices
documentation:
Returns a list of (x, y) tuples from a list of vertices in the format of:
'x1 y1 x2 y2 x3 y3 ... xn yn'

functions.name: papermodels.paper.annotations._group_vertices_str
documentation:
Returns a list of (x, y) tuples from a list of vertices in the format of:
'x1 y1 x2 y2 x3 y3 ... xn yn'

functions.name: papermodels.loads.utils.str_to_int
documentation:
Returns the string 's' converted into an integer if possible.
Returns the original string, 's', otherwise.

functions.name: papermodels.loads.utils.str_to_float
documentation:
Returns the string 's' converted into a float if possible.
Returns the original string, 's', otherwise.

functions.name: papermodels.loads.utils.read_csv_file
documentation:
Returns data contained in the file, 'filename' as a list of lists.
It is assumed that the data in the file is "csv-ish", meaning with
comma-separated values.

functions.name: papermodels.paper.pdf.load_pdf_annotations
documentation:
Returns a lists of pdf annotations keyed by page index.

'pdf_path': Path-like object representing the path to the PDF file to open.

functions.name: papermodels.paper.pdf.parse_content_stream
documentation:
Parses data in the content stream into an ordered dictionary.
Keys are operators. Values are operands.

Currently only parses non-path data reliably. Path data
is not parsed separately so, in the output dict, repetitive
operators are overwritten in the dict (e.g. multiple 'l'
operators will only have one entry).

functions.name: papermodels.paper.pdf.parse_html_text_content
documentation: Returns 'Python-formatted' text for html text in the annotation popup

functions.name: papermodels.paper.plot.plot_annotations
documentation:
Plots that annotations, 'annots' in matplotlib. Size and dpi can be adjusted
to make the plot bigger/smaller. Size is in inches and dpi stands for
"dots per inch". For a biggish plot, values of size=12, dpi=200 gives
good results.

functions.name: papermodels.paper.plot.xy_vertices
documentation: Returns a list of lists of floats to emulate a 2d numpy array of x, y values

modules.name: papermodels
documentation: Load networks

functions.name: papermodels.loads.load_factors.factor_load
documentation:
Returns the factored load given the load components (e.g. 'D_load', 'L_load', etc.)
and the load factors (e.g. 'D', 'L', etc.) provided.

functions.name: papermodels.loads.load_factors.max_factored_load
documentation:
Returns the max factored load from the given 'loads' and 'load_combos'.
'loads' is a dict with keys from {"D_load", "L_load", "S_load", "W_load", "E_load"}
    and float values representing load magnitudes. +ve/-ve is dependent on the designer's
    sign convention.
'load_combos' is a dict of str keys (representing the "names" of each load combination)
    with dict values representing load components and their factors, e.g.
    {"D": 1.25, "L": 1.5, "S": 1.0}

functions.name: papermodels.loads.load_factors.min_factored_load
documentation:
Returns the max factored load from the given 'loads' and 'load_combos'.
'loads' is a dict with keys from {"D_load", "L_load", "S_load", "W_load", "E_load"}
    and float values representing load magnitudes. +ve/-ve is dependent on the designer's
    sign convention.
'load_combos' is a dict of str keys (representing the "names" of each load combination)
    with dict values representing load components and their factors, e.g.
    {"D": 1.25, "L": 1.5, "S": 1.0}

functions.name: papermodels.loads.load_factors.envelope_max
documentation:
Returns the maximum factored array across all factored result arrays in 'result_arrays'.

'result_arrays': a dict of factored result arrays for an action on a specific framing member,
    keyed by load combo name. The result array values are a Nx2 array where the x-coordinates
    are in index 0 and the y-coordinates are in index 1

functions.name: papermodels.loads.load_factors.envelope_min
documentation:
Returns the minimum factored array across all factored result arrays in 'result_arrays'.

'result_arrays': a dict of factored result arrays for an action on a specific framing member,
    keyed by load combo name. The result array values are a Nx2 array where the x-coordinates
    are in index 0 and the y-coordinates are in index 1
Fancy, alternate implementation below...(used in min, afterwards)
def envelope_max(result_arrays: dict) -> list[list[float]]:
stacked_results = [result_array[1] for result_array in result_arrays.values()]
enveloped = [max(results_at_station) for results_at_station in zip(*stacked_results)]
first_key = list(result_arrays.keys())[0]
return [result_arrays[first_key][0], enveloped]

functions.name: papermodels.loads.load_distribution.project_polygon
documentation:
Returns the 'original' polygon, projected onto a horizontal line so that it maintains
the same area as 'original'.

If 'total_load' is given, the projected polygon is scaled so that it's total area
is equal to the value of 'total_load'.

If 'xy' is True, then instead of a Polygon object being returned, a list of
x-coordinates and a list of y-coordinates is returned.

functions.name: papermodels.loads.load_distribution.overlap_region_to_singularity
documentation: Returns a singularity function generated from 'ovlp'

functions.name: papermodels.loads.load_distribution.singularities_to_polygon
documentation:
Returns a Polygon in the shape of the singularity function.
If 'xy' is True, function returns a list of x-coords and a list of y-coords

functions.name: papermodels.loads.load_distribution.scale_singularity
documentation:
Returns a Singularity function that has been scaled to represent a trapezoid
with an area that is equal to the value of 'total_load'.

functions.name: papermodels.loads.load_distribution.calculate_scale_ratio
documentation: Returns a float representing the ratio of total_load to projected_poly

functions.name: papermodels.loads.load_distribution.get_range
documentation:
Returns a tuple representing the values of y0 and y1 calculated
from the information in 'ovlp' where y0 and y1 correlate with
the locations of x0 and x1.

functions.name: papermodels.loads.load_distribution.get_singularity_functions
documentation:
Returns a 2-tuple, each element a list of Singularity. The first element is the overlapping regions
of the convex hull of the polygon. The second element is the overlapping regions of the void spaces
within the convex hull.

functions.name: papermodels.loads.load_distribution.get_overlap_regions
documentation:
Returns a list of overlapping regions described by the vertices in the CONVEX polygon.
The overlapping regions are described as a list of Singularity functions.

functions.name: papermodels.loads.load_distribution.get_overlap_region
documentation:
Returns an Overlap object representing the overlapping portion of the line
segments a and b, represented by their respective points, p0 and p1.

functions.name: papermodels.loads.load_distribution.get_overlap_coords
documentation:
Returns a tuple representing the starting and ending x-coordinates of the region of overlap
between two line segments, a and b, defined solely by each line segment's start and end
x-coordinates, x0 and x1.

If no overlap exists, None is returned.

functions.name: papermodels.loads.load_distribution.get_slope_and_intercept
documentation:
Returns a tuple representing the slope and y-intercept of the line enclosing the
line segment represented by the points 'p0' and 'p1'.

p0 and p1 cannot describe a vertical line.

functions.name: papermodels.loads.load_distribution.get_void_regions
documentation:
Returns the void regions as a list of Polygon, if any
void regions exist within 'p'. If no void region is found,
then an empty list is returned.

A void region is defined as any region that does not lie
within the convex hull of 'p'.

classes.name: papermodels.loads.load_distribution.Overlap
documentation:
Represents attributes of two line segments, a and b, that are one above the other,
overlapping.
Each line segment is defined by the following attributes representing
each line as the equation y = mx + b
    x0, x1: starting and ending x coordinates
    m, b: slope and y-intercept

classes.name: papermodels.loads.load_distribution.Singularity

methods.name: papermodels.loads.load_distribution.Singularity.__call__
documentation:
Returns a value for 'y' calculated from 'x' and the attributes
stored in the singularity function class instance.

Represents a singularity function with customizable attributes. The
Singularity can be called like a function to generate a 'y' value
from a given 'x' value.

The singularity function is assumed to be linear and defined over a
certain domain between x0 and x1, a slope of m, and with an initial
y-value of y0. The resulting y-value for y1 will be rounded to
'precision'.

This class is defined as a callable class instead of as a function
so that the attributes can be altered as required for appropriate
scaling of the singularity function after it has been defined.

functions.name: papermodels.geometry.geom_ops.get_intersection
documentation: Returns the details of the intersection

functions.name: papermodels.geometry.geom_ops.get_joist_extents
documentation:
Returns the extents for the supports "A" and "B". Each extent is represented by a tuple of
Point objects which represent the "i" (start) and "j" (end) locations on the supports
given in 'joist_supports' which support the 'joist_prototype'.

'joist_supports' is a list of two LineString where each LineString only has one line segment
    (the relevant line segment which provides the support to 'joist_prototype')

functions.name: papermodels.geometry.geom_ops.get_cantilever_segments
documentation:
Returns a dictionary containing the cantilever lengths over-hanging supports "A" and
"B", respectively. Returns a length of 0.0 if the length is less than the tolerance.

functions.name: papermodels.geometry.geom_ops.get_system_bounds
documentation:
Returns the minx, miny, maxx, maxy bounding box of all the LineStrings in 'joist_supports',
taken as a group.

functions.name: papermodels.geometry.geom_ops.get_magnitude
documentation:
Returns the distance of the "min" and "max" coordinates described in 'bounds'

'bounds': represents the minx, miny, maxx, maxy values of the "min" and "max" coordinates

functions.name: papermodels.geometry.geom_ops.get_joist_locations
documentation: Returns a list of location offsets (starting from 0.0)

functions.name: papermodels.geometry.geom_ops.get_direction_vector
documentation:
Returns a numpy array representing the normalized +ve direction vector of the LineString 'ls'.

'ls': A LineString with two or more points. If there are more than two points, it
    is assumed that all points are co-linear.

functions.name: papermodels.geometry.geom_ops.determine_support_order
documentation:
Returns a dict identifying which support is "A" and which is "B".

The "A" and "B" supports are arranged so that the vector of the joist spanning
between them is going to be in the +ve direction (positive X bias). See the
docstring for get_start_end_nodes for more explanation of the +ve vector direction.

functions.name: papermodels.geometry.geom_ops.get_start_end_nodes
documentation:
Returns the "i" and "j" nodes for the coordinates comprising 'ls' in such a way that
it produces a +ve vector when j_node - i_node is performed. See docstring for
order_nodes_positive for more information about the +ve vector.

'ls': A LineString whose points are assumed to be co-linear if there are more than two.

functions.name: papermodels.geometry.geom_ops.order_nodes_positive
documentation:
Returns the 'i_node' and 'j_node' in the order of "A" and "B" node where "A"
and "B" node generate a +ve vector when B - A.

A +ve vector is a vector that has an angle, theta, when measured from horizontal, with
the following range: -pi / 2 < theta <= pi/2. This can also be thought of as a vector
with a "positive x bias" because such a vector will never point in the -ve x direction.

functions.name: papermodels.geometry.geom_ops.project_node
documentation:
Returns a Point representing 'node' projected along 'vector' for a distance of
'magnitude'.

'node': a point in 2D or 3D space
'vector': a normalized vector in 2D or 3D space
'magnitude': the distance along 'vector' that 'node' should be projected

functions.name: papermodels.geometry.geom_ops.rotate_90
documentation:
Rotate the vector components, 'x1' and 'y1' by 90 degrees.

'precision': round result to this many decimal places
'ccw': if True, rotate counter-clockwise (clockwise, otherwise)

classes.name: papermodels.datatypes.geometry_graph.GeometryGraph

methods.name: papermodels.datatypes.geometry_graph.GeometryGraph.from_elements
documentation:
Returns a LoadGraph (networkx.DiGraph) based upon the intersections and correspondents
of the 'elements'.

methods.name: papermodels.datatypes.geometry_graph.GeometryGraph.hash_nodes
documentation:
Returns None. Sets the value of self.node_hash based on the hashed values of
the nodes.

A class to represent a connected geometry system in a graph. Inherits from networkx.DiGraph
and adds a .node_hash attribute for storing a hash of all the nodes.

Can be used to generate a LoadGraph.

The node_hash is how changes to the graph nodes can be tracked.

functions.name: papermodels.units.parsing.parse_unit_system
documentation:
Returns a UnitSystem enum based on the provided 'unit_designation'.

'unit_designation': one of {'kPa', 'MPa', 'GPa', 'psf', 'ksf', 'psi', 'ksi'}

The unit designation is considered enough to describe units of both force and
distance in a single three-character string. Their meanings are as follows:

kPa = kN / m2
MPa = N / mm2
GPa = kN / mm2
psf = lbs / ft2
psi = lbs / inch2
ksf = kips / ft2
ksi = kips / inch2

functions.name: papermodels.units.parsing.parse_unit_string
documentation:
Returns a Physical to represent the physical quantity described by 'unit_string'

'unit_string': a string formatted as "{number} {unit}" where unit must match a variable
name within the forallpeople.environment dictionary. If the unit is not found, a
KeyError is raised.

functions.name: papermodels.units.parsing.parse_exponent
documentation:
Returns a tuple that includes the exponent described on the 'unit_value'.

e.g. unit_value = "mm4" -> ("mm", 4)
     unit_value = "kN" -> ("kN", 1)

functions.name: papermodels.units.parsing.parse_arch_notation
documentation:
Returns a string representing the nominal value of 'dimension_string' in inches.
e.g.
    1'6 -> "18.0 inch"
    0'4 5/8 -> "4.625 inch"

functions.name: papermodels.units.parsing.convert_unit_string
documentation:
Returns a float of the 'unit_string' after it has been normalized into the
units of 'unit_system'.

functions.name: papermodels.units.parsing.get_quantity_type
documentation:
Returns one of
{'length', 'force', 'pressure', 'line', 'torque', None} in correspondence
with the .dimensions attribute of 'p'.

Dimensions of 'p' that are powers of the above quantity types (such as
area or volume) are all considered whatever the "base" type is.

e.g. 'length' applies to lengths, areas, volumes, moments of area, etc.

functions.name: papermodels.datatypes.utils.class_representation
documentation:
Returns a generic repr string for a given 'object' where 'object' is a
"data class"-like object.

functions.name: papermodels.datatypes.loads.parse_load_text
documentation: Returns a dict representing the parsed load data present in 'text'

classes.name: papermodels.datatypes.loads.LoadElement

methods.name: papermodels.datatypes.loads.LoadElement.from_parsed_annotation
documentation:
Returns a LoadElement based on the data from 'annot' and 'annotation_props'
which is a dictionary of the values resulting from the function
papermodels.paper.annotations.parse_annotations.

'annot': the Annotation
'annotation_props': dict with keys "type" and "geometry", where "type" 
    corresponds to the legend data associated with the annotation that
    would have been extracted during the parse_annotations function.

    For loads, the value for "type" should be a str formatted like the following:
    "Load
    [occupancy=<occupancy_name>]
    [[load_component=load_magnitude]
     [load_component=load_magnitude], etc. ]

     e.g. 
     Load
     occupancy=Residential

     e.g.
     Load
     D=34 psf
     L=4.8
     S=1.2 kPa

     The load component must be on the left-hand side of an "=" 
     and the load magnitude can either be a str or float representing
     a magnitude. If not convertible to a float, the magnitude will need
     to be parsed.
'project_occupancies': a dict describine occupancy names and their load components
'unit_system': one of {'psf', 'ksf', 'psi', 'ksi', 'kPa', 'MPa', 'GPa'}

methods.name: papermodels.datatypes.loads.LoadElement.parse_load_components
documentation:
Returns a copy of self.load_components where the values of the keys have
been converted into a float with appropriate scaling as per their units
and the overall unit system.
Represents a loading area extracted from a markup

classes.name: papermodels.datatypes.loads.LoadArray

methods.name: papermodels.datatypes.loads.LoadArray.from_annotations
documentation:
Returns a LoadArray-style dict populated with LoadElements.

parsed_load_annotations is a dict with Annotation objects as keys
and dict of values. The values dict has keys, "type" and "geometry".
The value of the "type" key will match the annotation's corresponding
legend entry so the use of the user-defined legend will persist.

A type alias to represent LoadElements extracted from the paper model.

The actual dictionary is also stored under the 'data' attribute, as per the 
conventional UserDict behaviour.

The dictionary stored within should be in the following structure.
    {
        "page_number_1": [
            load_element_1,
            load_element_2,
            load_element_3
        ],
        "page_number_2": [
            load_element_3,
            load_element_4
        ]
    }

functions.name: papermodels.datatypes.element.get_tag_type
documentation:
Returns the prefix portion of 'this_element_tag'. The prefix portion is the
alphabetical portion of the tag at the beginning.

functions.name: papermodels.datatypes.element.get_elements_by_page
documentation: Returns 'elements' sorted by page

functions.name: papermodels.datatypes.element.get_normalized_coordinate
documentation:
Returns a normalized x-coordinate for the given 'intersection_point' as it is located on the geometry
of 'element'.

functions.name: papermodels.datatypes.element.get_structured_model_data

classes.name: papermodels.datatypes.element.Element

methods.name: papermodels.datatypes.element.Element.get_intersection
documentation:
Returns the corresponding Point object for 'tag' if 'tag' is in the tuple of
self.intersections. Returns None if not.

A dataclass to generically represent a structural element on a PDF page
whether it is a beam, column, joist, or otherwise.

Note: each Element has no "knowledge" of its intersections and correspondents.
They are generated in a separate process and are not part of the capability
of the class. These attributes are simply to keep track of pre-discovered
intersections and correspondents.

'tag': str, represent a unique name for this element, as per the designer's preference
'type': str, describing what "type" of element it is. This is not an enumeration
    and can take any designer-defined value. It is for user-level categorization.
'page': int, describing the page index of the PDF that this element is found on
'geometry': the shapely geometry that represents this element in 2D space
'intersections': list[tuple[str, Point]], where each 2-tuple represents the tag
    of the other Element that this element intersects with on plan and the point
    of intersection. Intersections exist only between elements that are on the same PDF page
    and thus are used to describe connections that occur on the horizontal plane.
'correspondents': list[str], where each item in the list represents the tag of another
    Element that is approximately in the exact same position as this element but on the
    adjacent page. Correspondents exist only between elements that are on adjacent PDF
    pages and thus are used to describe connections that occur on the vertical plane
    such as the the top and bottom of a wall or column (the bottom may be on page 0 and
    the top may be on page 1)
'page_label': A designer-defined str label for the page (e.g. "Ground Floor" or "L03", etc.)

methods.name: papermodels.datatypes.element_models.ElementModel.determine_loads
documentation:
Returns None. Performs a geometric intersection with each load present
in 'loads' to determine if any of the loads intersect with the 
self.trib_area of the element.

classes.name: papermodels.datatypes.element_models.JoistArrayModel

methods.name: papermodels.datatypes.element_models.JoistArrayModel.generate_joist
documentation:
Returns i, j coordinates of the joist in the JoistArray at the position
of 'index'. Raises IndexError if 'index' is not within the joist array
extents given the spacing.

'index': joists are numbered from 0 (first joist, at joist extent) and
    go to n, the last joist in the array.

methods.name: papermodels.datatypes.element_models.JoistArrayModel.get_extent_edge
documentation:
Gets the "joist" that would exist at the edge of the array

'edge': one of {'start', 'end'}

methods.name: papermodels.datatypes.element_models.JoistArrayModel.get_joist_trib_widths
documentation:
Returns the trib widths of the the joist at 'index'. The trib
widths are a tuple representing the left and right width,
respectively.

methods.name: papermodels.datatypes.element_models.JoistArrayModel.generate_trib_area
documentation:
Returns a tuple of Polygon representing the tributary area of the 'joist' based on the
given 'trib_widths'

Models a spread of joists over a region where the distance
between the supports may vary linearly.

functions.name: papermodels.datatypes.joist_models.get_joist_extents
documentation:
Returns the extents for the supports "A" and "B". Each extent is represented by a tuple of
Point objects which represent the "i" (start) and "j" (end) locations on the supports
given in 'joist_supports' which support the 'joist_prototype'.

'joist_supports' is a list of two LineString where each LineString only has one line segment
    (the relevant line segment which provides the support to 'joist_prototype')

functions.name: papermodels.datatypes.joist_models.get_cantilever_segments
documentation:
Returns a dictionary containing the cantilever lengths over-hanging supports "A" and
"B", respectively. Returns a length of 0.0 if the length is less than the tolerance.

functions.name: papermodels.datatypes.joist_models.get_system_bounds
documentation:
Returns the minx, miny, maxx, maxy bounding box of all the LineStrings in 'joist_supports',
taken as a group.

functions.name: papermodels.datatypes.joist_models.get_magnitude
documentation:
Returns the distance of the "min" and "max" coordinates described in 'bounds'

'bounds': represents the minx, miny, maxx, maxy values of the "min" and "max" coordinates

functions.name: papermodels.datatypes.joist_models.get_joist_locations
documentation: Returns a list of location offsets (starting from 0.0)

functions.name: papermodels.datatypes.joist_models.get_direction_vector
documentation:
Returns a numpy array representing the normalized +ve direction vector of the LineString 'ls'.

'ls': A LineString with two or more points. If there are more than two points, it
    is assumed that all points are co-linear.

functions.name: papermodels.datatypes.joist_models.determine_support_order
documentation:
Returns a dict identifying which support is "A" and which is "B".

The "A" and "B" supports are arranged so that the vector of the joist spanning
between them is going to be in the +ve direction (positive X bias). See the
docstring for get_start_end_nodes for more explanation of the +ve vector direction.

functions.name: papermodels.datatypes.joist_models.get_start_end_nodes
documentation:
Returns the "i" and "j" nodes for the coordinates comprising 'ls' in such a way that
it produces a +ve vector when j_node - i_node is performed. See docstring for
order_nodes_positive for more information about the +ve vector.

'ls': A LineString whose points are assumed to be co-linear if there are more than two.

functions.name: papermodels.datatypes.joist_models.order_nodes_positive
documentation:
Returns the 'i_node' and 'j_node' in the order of "A" and "B" node where "A"
and "B" node generate a +ve vector when B - A.

A +ve vector is a vector that has an angle, theta, when measured from horizontal, with
the following range: -pi / 2 < theta <= pi/2. This can also be thought of as a vector
with a "positive x bias" because such a vector will never point in the -ve x direction.

functions.name: papermodels.datatypes.joist_models.project_node
documentation:
Returns a Point representing 'node' projected along 'vector' for a distance of
'magnitude'.

'node': a point in 2D or 3D space
'vector': a normalized vector in 2D or 3D space
'magnitude': the distance along 'vector' that 'node' should be projected

functions.name: papermodels.datatypes.joist_models.rotate_90
documentation:
Rotate the vector components, 'x1' and 'y1' by 90 degrees.

'precision': round result to this many decimal places
'ccw': if True, rotate counter-clockwise (clockwise, otherwise)

methods.name: papermodels.datatypes.joist_models.JoistArrayModel.generate_joist
documentation:
Returns i, j coordinates of the joist in the JoistArray at the position
of 'index'. Raises IndexError if 'index' is not within the joist array
extents given the spacing.

'index': joists are numbered from 0 (first joist, at joist extent) and
    go to n, the last joist in the array.

methods.name: papermodels.datatypes.joist_models.JoistArrayModel.get_extent_edge
documentation:
Gets the "joist" that would exist at the edge of the array

'edge': one of {'start', 'end'}

methods.name: papermodels.datatypes.joist_models.JoistArrayModel.get_joist_trib_widths
documentation:
Returns the trib widths of the the joist at 'index'. The trib
widths are a tuple representing the left and right width,
respectively.

methods.name: papermodels.datatypes.joist_models.JoistArrayModel.generate_trib_area
documentation:
Returns a tuple of Polygon representing the tributary area of the 'joist' based on the

Models a spread of joists over a region where the distance
between the supports may vary linearly.

classes.name: papermodels.datatypes.joist_models.Joist
documentation:
Models a joist with a uniform load of
'w' on all spans of the joist that exist.

             w
||||||||||||||||||||||||||||
----------------------------
    ^                  ^
    R1                 R2
< a ><      span      >< b >

functions.name: papermodels.datatypes.load_graph.element_to_beam_model
documentation: Returns an FEModel3D for the data in 'element'

functions.name: papermodels.datatypes.load_graph.element_to_joist_array
documentation: Returns a Joist object based on the data in 'element'
span = r1_geom.distance(r2_geom)
a_cantilever = round(abs(i_end.distance(r1_geom)), 6)
b_cantilever = round(abs(r2_geom.distance(j_end)), 6)
return Joist(span, a_cantilever, b_cantilever)

classes.name: papermodels.datatypes.load_graph.LoadGraph

methods.name: papermodels.datatypes.load_graph.LoadGraph.hash_nodes
documentation:
Returns None. Sets the value of self.node_hash based on the hashed values of
the nodes.

methods.name: papermodels.datatypes.load_graph.LoadGraph.compile_load_distribution_model
documentation: Returns None. Adds an empty 'load_distribution' dict to each node.

methods.name: papermodels.datatypes.load_graph.LoadGraph.compile_distribution_functions
documentation:
Returns None. Populates the empty 'load_distribution' dict in 
each node.

A class to represent a load path in a graph. Inherits from networkx.DiGraph
and adds a .node_hash attribute for storing a hash of all the nodes.

The node_hash is how changes to the graph nodes can be tracked.

functions.name: papermodels.db.data_model.class_representation

"data class"-like object.

functions.name: papermodels.datatypes.analysis_models.get_node_locations
documentation:
Returns a dict representing the node names and node coordinates required for
a beam with support locations at 'support_locations' and a beam of length of
'beam_length'.

Each node name and node location is unique. Nodes are numbered sequentially from
left to right starting at "N0".

functions.name: papermodels.datatypes.analysis_models.calc_shear_modulus
documentation:
Returns the shear modulus based on Poisson's ratio and
the elastic modulus

'E': Elastic modulus
'nu': Poisson's ratio

methods.name: papermodels.datatypes.analysis_models.PyNiteFEModel.create_model
documentation: Returns a FEModel3D of a beam that has the attributes described in 'element_data' and 'A', 'J', 'nu', and 'rho'.

methods.name: papermodels.datatypes.analysis_models.PyNiteFEModel.add_load
documentation:
Adds one load to the model in self.analysis_model.
Sets self.analyzed to False to force a re-analysis.
